whatItDoes: Evaluate mathematical expressions with support for basic operators (+, -, *, /, ^, %), functions (sqrt, abs, sin, cos, tan, log, ln, floor, ceil, round), constants (pi, e), and factorial. Includes a history of previous calculations and quick-access example expressions.
whyItExists: Performing quick math calculations with functions and constants is cumbersome in standard calculators; this tool provides a developer-friendly interface that supports programming-style math expressions with immediate results and history.
whoShouldUse: Developers, students, engineers, and anyone who needs to quickly evaluate mathematical expressions with functions and constants without opening a full calculator application.
useCases: Quickly evaluate a math expression during coding or debugging
Calculate trigonometric values like sin(pi/2) or cos(0)
Test mathematical formulas before implementing them in code
Use as a quick reference for supported math functions
Review previous calculations in the history panel
instructions: Type a mathematical expression into the input field (e.g., 2 * (3 + 4) / 7)
Press Enter or click the Evaluate button to compute the result
The result is displayed below the input with full precision
Click any example expression to load and evaluate it instantly
Click any history entry to reload that expression for re-evaluation
Use the function reference panel to see all supported operations
bestPractices: Use parentheses to ensure correct order of operations
Remember that trigonometric functions use radians, not degrees
Use log() for base-10 logarithm and ln() for natural logarithm
The ^ operator is for exponentiation, not XOR
Check the function reference panel for the exact function names and syntax
commonMistakes: Using degrees instead of radians for trigonometric functions (sin(pi/2) = 1, not sin(90))
Forgetting parentheses in complex expressions leading to wrong order of operations
Using ^ for XOR instead of exponentiation
Expecting very large factorials to compute quickly (5! is fine, 100! will overflow)
faq: What order of operations does the evaluator follow? — Standard mathematical order: parentheses first, then exponents, then multiplication/division, then addition/subtraction.
Can I use variables? — No, this tool evaluates pure mathematical expressions only. Use a programming environment for variable-based calculations.
Why does sin(90) not give 1? — Trigonometric functions use radians. Use sin(pi/2) for the sine of 90 degrees.
What happens with division by zero? — The evaluator will return an error message indicating the expression is invalid.
